texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We study some properties of the growth rate of L p A , F q , that is, the language of words over the alphabet A avoiding the set of forbidden factors F . We first provide a sufficient condition on F and A for the growth of L p A , F q to be boundedly supermultiplicative. That is, there exist constants C ą 0 and α ě 0, such that for all n , the number of words of length n in L p A , F q is between α n and Cα n . In some settings, our condition provides a way to compute C , which implies that α , the growth rate of the language, is also computable whenever our condition holds.
texts.text: We study some properties of the growth rate of L p A , F q , that is, the language of words over the alphabet A avoiding the set of forbidden factors F . We first provide a sufficient condition on F and A for the growth of L p A , F q to be boundedly supermultiplicative. That is, there exist constants C ą 0 and α ě 0, such that for all n , the number of words of length n in L p A , F q is between α n and Cα n . In some settings, our condition provides a way to compute C , which implies that α , the growth rate of the language, is also computable whenever our condition holds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also apply our technique to the specific setting of power-free words where the argument can be slightly refined to provide better bounds. Finally, we apply a similar idea to F -free circular words and in particular we make progress toward a conjecture of Shur about the number of square-free circular words.

texts.orig: The readers should note that (2) as well as other equations in the article involve | f | for f P F . A small issue is that different sets F of forbidden factors may give the same language L p A , F q . Therefore, most bounds in this article depend on the choice of F . However, it is not hard to see that we can always choose F to be minimal, in the sense that each factor in F avoids all the other factors in F . For a given language L , this choice of F always gives the best bounds among all the choices. However, there are other ways to improve this choice of F . For instance, it is known that the growth of infinitely extendable words from L is the same as the growth of L which can be used to obtain even better bounds.

texts.orig: With more information about the set F , one can improve the conclusions of Theorem 1. In particular, in the case of p -powers or patterns, we have a stronger version of this theorem [3]. Similarly, we can also obtain a stronger version of our result in the setting of avoidability of p -powers. In particular, it allows us to prove (4) for the language of square-free words over an alphabet of size 5 for some constant C . The particular attention given to p -powers is motivated by the fact that this subject received a lot of attention in combinatorics on words since the seminal work of Thue [16].

texts.orig: We use a similar technique once more to give a partial resolution to a conjecture by Shur about the growth of the number of circular-square-free words [17]. Circular words can be seen as cyclically ordered finite sequences of letters. That is, we interpret the word as written on a circle, and we read factors along this circle. More formally, we say that two words u and v are conjugate if there are words s, t such that u ' st and v ' ts . The cyclic word ˝ u , is the set of conjugates of the word u , and we say that ˝ u avoids a factor f if for all conjugates v P ˝ u , we have v avoids f . In [17], Shur proved that the number of square-free circular words of length n and minimal squares of length 2 n (squares that do not contain another square as a proper factor) grows at the same rate [17, Proposition 1]. texts.orig: From computer experiments, we learn that the growth rate for the set of ternary square-free circular words is approximately 1 . 3. This growth rate obviously cannot exceed the growth rate for the set of ternary square-free ordinary words. For the latter one, a nearly exact value is now known [18]. It is 1 . 30176 . . . , so it is quite intriguing whether the two considered growth rates coincide.

texts.orig: The question of estimating the number of ternary square-free circular words was also explicitly asked in [19]. We prove in Theorem 14 that Shur's conjecture is true for any alphabet of size at least 5, but we leave open the cases of the alphabets of size 4 and 3. The question of F -free circular words has also been studied in a context more general than squares [20, 21], so we also provide a general sufficient condition on F in Theorem 16 that ensures the existence of F -free circular words.

texts.orig: We have proven that over any alphabet of size at least 5 the number of square-free circular words has the same growth rate as the number of square-free words. The question remains open for alphabets of size 3 and 4. It was first proven by Currie [22] that there are square-free circular ternary words of all lengths other than 5 , 7 , 9 , 10 , 14 , 17. The existence of these six lengths implies that we cannot hope for the existence of a positive constant C such that for all n , | ˝ L n pt 0 , 1 , 2 u , 2 q| ě C | L n pt 0 , 1 , 2 u , 2 q| . If it is possible to use the same technique for the case of ternary words, it probably requires handling all these cases, which probably entails a computer assisted proof. It might be easier to deal with the alphabet of size 4, but we do not know how to do it.

texts.orig: Circular-free, supermultiplicativity and the Restivo-Salemi property Note that C from Theorem 16 (resp. Corollary 15) is the same as in Theorem 3 (resp. Corollary 13). However, these constants are not optimal in general, but the optimal constants might be equal as well for many well-behaved languages. Roughly speaking, these two constants give a lower bound on the probability that two valid words can be concatenated without creating a forbidden factor. In Theorem 3 the two words are taken independently, while in Theorem 16 these two words are the prefix and suffix of the same word. Our intuition is that for a random p -free word the prefix and suffix of length n are almost independent if the word is long enough. This motivates the following definitions and problem. texts.orig: It appears that this question might be related to whether or not the language has the Restivo-Salemi property. For any language L , we let e p L q be the set of words that are infinitely extendable in both directions, that is, e p L q ' t w P L : @ n, D u, v P A n , uwv P L u . Following [18], we say that a language has the Restivo-Salemi property, 2 if for all u, v P e p L q there exists w P A ˚ such that uwv P e p L q . In particular, Shur conjectured that any language defined by avoidance of p -powers has the Restivo-Salemi property [18]. Recent progress on this conjecture include the cases | A | ě 2 and p ' 3 [24] and | A | ě 3 and p ě 5 [25]. The kind of random-like structural properties of a language that imply the Restivo-Salemi property might be related to the ones that imply the equality in Problem 1. The technique in this article could also be useful to solve some of the missing cases of Shur's conjecture on the Restivo-Salemi property of p -power free languages, but we leave this open as we were not able to make any progress ourselves on this problem.
